channel.title: The Independent Ledger
channel.description: Independent reporting on AI, media and money.

item.title: AI content licensing and creator compensation: where the fight stands
item.dc:creator: Maya Okonkwo
item.content:encoded: AI content licensing now turns on three contested questions: whether training on copyrighted work is fair use, how creators are compensated when models ground answers in their work, and whether per-use settlement can replace blanket licensing. Advocates of per-citation pay argue a source should earn every time an agent grounds an answer in it, not once at training time. The hard part remains proving reuse and attaching payment to it.

item.title: Why subscriptions priced out the five-cent article
item.dc:creator: Daniel Reyes
item.content:encoded: For as long as a payment could not clear below roughly thirty cents after fees, a five-cent article could not be sold on its own, so everything was bundled into a monthly subscription. Removing the payment floor makes the smallest unit sellable for the first time, which changes what independent creators can charge for.

item.title: Citizen journalism funding when readers pay per story
item.dc:creator: Priya Nair
item.content:encoded: Local and citizen journalism has struggled because a single story rarely justifies a subscription, and ad revenue collapsed. Per-story micropayments let a reporter earn from the thousand places a story travels, with readers paying a few cents for the pieces they actually open rather than pledging monthly to outlets they rarely read.

item.title: Music royalties split by what listeners actually played
item.dc:creator: Tomas Vidal
item.content:encoded: Streaming platforms pool every subscription and pay artists pro rata, so listeners mostly fund whoever is globally popular rather than who they played. User-centric royalties split a listener's payment across the artists they actually heard, settled per listen, with play-gating so an early skip costs nothing. Direct sub-cent settlement makes the model practical.

item.title: Per-image licensing and instant photographer payouts
item.dc:creator: Hannah Berg
item.content:encoded: Licensing a single photograph has been clumsy and slow, so most images are used without payment or attribution. Attaching a price to an individual image and settling instantly to the photographer named in the file turns licensing into a one-click action, and recursive splits pay every collaborator the file's metadata already records.

item.title: Streaming payments by the second for live media
item.dc:creator: Marcus Lowe
item.content:encoded: Live media is continuous, so the natural unit of payment is the second, not a flat ticket. Continuous-authorization streaming lets a viewer approve a spending rate and pay only for the time they were present, with revenue accruing live and splitting across everyone on the stream. Sub-second finality and gasless batching make per-second settlement viable.

item.title: Attribution metadata as the payout rule for collaborative work
item.dc:creator: Maya Okonkwo
item.content:encoded: Collaborative content already records who did what: track credits, photo provenance, editor lists. Reading that lineage graph turns attribution metadata into settlement logic, so a remix of a remix pays every ancestor automatically and an edited photo pays the original photographer. Recursive, automatic splits replace the manual reconciliation creators do today.

item.title: Surface-code roadmaps for 2026 (control source)
item.dc:creator: Lin Wei
item.content:encoded: Surface-code thresholds and logical qubit overheads, with 2026 roadmap comparisons across hardware platforms. Included as an off-topic control to test the agent's relevance filtering.

item.title: A field guide to alpine wildflowers (control source)
item.dc:creator: Greta Olsen
item.content:encoded: Identifying alpine wildflowers by petal count, leaf shape, and elevation band. Included as an off-topic control the agent should decline for finance and media questions.
